claims.text: Overvaluation of tech companies contributed to the dot-com crash.
Lack of regulation in the tech industry contributed to the dot-com crash.
Speculation in dotcom or internet-based businesses caused the dot-com bubble and subsequent crash.

decision.synthesis: The dot-com crash was caused by a combination of factors.

evidence.content: The dot com crash also had broader economic consequences, as the decline in tech stocks led to a decline in consumer confidence and spending.

evidence.content: The dot-com boom of 1995–2000 (and ultimate bust in 2001–2002) was a period of large, rapid, and ultimately unsustainable increases in the stock market—specifically in the valuation of shares in Internet service and technology companies, then commonly referred to as “dot-com” companies, including fledgling businesses, or “start-ups,” with little or no record of profitability or with unrealistic business models. During the dot-com bubble, the technology-dominated Nasdaq Composite index (a representation of the total value of the outstanding shares of companies listed on the Nasdaq stock exchange) rose nearly sevenfold from 743 to 5,048, reflecting the early enthusiasm of investors in dot-com enterprises and the willingness of venture capitalists to finance the initial public offerings (IPOs) of Internet start-ups, many of whose share prices then skyrocketed. As do all financial bubbles, the dot-com bubble finally burst.

additional.query: Define the scope and timeline of the dot-com crash

During the dot-com bubble, the technology-dominated Nasdaq Composite index (a representation of the total value of the outstanding shares of companies listed on the Nasdaq stock exchange) rose nearly sevenfold from 743 to 5,048, reflecting the early enthusiasm of investors in dot-com enterprises and the willingness of venture capitalists to finance the initial public offerings (IPOs) of Internet start-ups, many of whose share prices then skyrocketed. As do all financial bubbles, the dot-com bubble finally burst.

evidence.content: A stock market bubble that was caused by speculation in dotcom or internet-based businesses from 1995 to 2000. The dotcom bubble is a stock market bubble that was caused by speculation in dotcom or internet-based businesses from 1995 to 2000. The dotcom bubble’s origins can be traced to the launch of the World Wide Web in 1989, the subsequent establishment of internet and tech-based start-up companies during the 1990s, and rising momentum as the decade came to its end. The bursting of the bubble caused market panic through massive sell-offs of dotcom company stocks, driving their values further down, and by 2002, investor losses were estimated at around $5 trillion. Most tech and internet companies that held IPOs during the dotcom era were highly overvalued due to increasing demand and a lack of solid valuation models. Money pouring into tech and internet company start-ups by venture capitalists and other investors was one of the major causes of the dotcom bubble.

The dotcom bubble, also known as the Internet bubble, grew out of a combination of the presence of speculative or fad-based investing, the abundance of venture capital funding for start-ups, and the failure of dotcoms to turn a profit. While companies like Intel, Cisco, and Oracle drove growth in tech, upstart dotcom companies sparked the stock market surge starting in 1995. The 2000 stock market crash was a direct result of the bursting of the dotcom bubble. couple managing finance and investment online, analyzing stock market trades with mobile app on smartphone:max_bytes(150000):strip_icc()/Primary-Image-how-to-trade-forex-7499569-44467e75edf24375a03f63fe6f791310.jpg). evidence.content: The Dot-com bubble refers to a significant economic phenomenon that occurred in the late 1990s and early 2000s, characterized by the rapid rise and subsequent fall of internet-based companies' stock prices. Interest Rates;NASDAQ Dive Prompts Dot-Com Crash;September 11 terrorist attacks' impact on business;Dot-coms;Amazon (company). Interest Rates;NASDAQ Dive Prompts Dot-Com Crash;September 11 terrorist attacks' impact on business;Dot-coms;Amazon (company). In what has been dubbed the dot-com bubble, the stock prices of Internet companies soared during this time as investors forecasted the vast potential of the **World Wide Web**. When many of the dot-com companies failed to thrive, investors lost millions of dollars.*. Spurred by low interest rates and rising stock prices, investors provided a glut of venture capital to countless Internet companies. Even though many considered the possibility of another dot-com bubble, most experts agreed that the surge in stock prices was significantly different from the increases seen in the early 2000s. “The Dot-Com Bubble Bursts.” *New York Times*.

raw_payload.content: Dotcom Bubble:max_bytes(150000):strip_icc()/dotcom-bubble.asp-FINAL-97b80a4f3ae44b38bef25defa02127a5.png). Dotcom Bubble:max_bytes(150000):strip_icc()/dotcom-bubble.asp-FINAL-97b80a4f3ae44b38bef25defa02127a5.png). The dotcom bubble, also known as the Internet bubble, grew out of a combination of the presence of speculative or fad-based investing, the abundance of venture capital funding for start-ups, and the failure of dotcoms to turn a profit. While companies like Intel, Cisco, and Oracle drove growth in tech, upstart dotcom companies sparked the stock market surge starting in 1995. The 2000 stock market crash was a direct result of the bursting of the dotcom bubble. couple managing finance and investment online, analyzing stock market trades with mobile app on smartphone:max_bytes(150000):strip_icc()/Primary-Image-how-to-trade-forex-7499569-44467e75edf24375a03f63fe6f791310.jpg). steps.success_criteria: Find at least 3 significant economic factors

steps.description: Analyze the role of speculation and hype in the dot-com bubble
steps.expected_output: Insights into how speculation and hype fueled the bubble and its eventual collapse

steps.description: Examine the impact of regulatory failures on the crash

steps.success_criteria: Find at least 1 significant instance of regulatory failure
